dating.display: composed c. 1500–1000 BCE; transmitted orally; oldest surviving manuscripts c. 14th century CE
dating.basis: Linguistic stratigraphy (family books older than books 1 and 10), correlation with the Mitanni Indo-Aryan loanwords (c. 1400 BCE), and river geography. There is no direct physical evidence of the composition period: the text survived by oral transmission.

positions.position: Mainstream philology: core composition c. 1500–1000 BCE, Punjab region
positions.holders: Witzel, Jamison & Brereton, and most Vedic philologists
positions.evidence: Language stage close to Mitanni Indo-Aryan and Avestan; geography of the Sapta Sindhu; internal stratification.

positions.position: Substantially earlier composition (3rd millennium BCE or before)
positions.holders: Indigenist / Out-of-India writers; some astronomical-dating arguments (e.g. readings of Vedic star lore)
positions.evidence: Proposed astronomical references and Sarasvatī-river readings; mainstream philology finds these inconclusive. Listed here because the atlas states competing positions rather than deciding by fiat where method itself is disputed.

summary: 1,028 hymns in ten maṇḍalas — the oldest stratum of the Vedic corpus and among the oldest religious texts in continuous liturgical use anywhere. Preserved for roughly three millennia by a redundant oral architecture (pada, krama, jaṭā, ghana pāṭhas) that encodes the text several ways at once so that errors are detectable — a checksum, built of human memory.
whyItMatters: It is the root document of the Vedic tradition and the reference point for nearly every dating debate in this atlas. Its transmission system is itself a landmark of information engineering: the recitation survives essentially uncorrupted across regions that lost contact with each other centuries ago.
honestyNote: The tag is 'evolved': composition spans centuries and the absolute dates are inferential. Claims of laser-precise dates in either direction — '1500 BCE, settled' or '7000 BCE, proven' — overstate what the evidence supports.

throughLines.to: Study of high-fidelity oral transmission; UNESCO-recognized living heritage
chain: Vedic pāṭha recitation systems encode the text redundantly (word-by-word, chained, braided forms)
Regional recitation lineages preserved matching texts independently for centuries
UNESCO proclaimed the tradition of Vedic chanting a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity (2003/2008 list)

sources.label: Staal, F., "The Fidelity of Oral Tradition and the Origins of Science" (North-Holland, 1986)
UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage: Tradition of Vedic Chanting
Jamison, S. & Brereton, J., The Rigveda: The Earliest Religious Poetry of India (OUP, 2014)
Witzel, M., "Vedas and Upaniṣads", in The Blackwell Companion to Hinduism (2003)
Staal, F., "The Fidelity of Oral Tradition and the Origins of Science" (1986)
Anthony, D., The Horse, the Wheel, and Language (Princeton, 2007) — Mitanni synchronism
Bryant, E., The Quest for the Origins of Vedic Culture (OUP, 2001) — survey of the dating debate on all sides
